2119:       subroutine zran3(z,n1,n2,n3,nx,ny,k)


[...]


2149:       a1 = power( a, nx, 1, 0 )


2150:       a2 = power( a, nx, ny, 0 )


2151: 


2152:       call zero3(z,n1,n2,n3)


2153: 


2154: !      i = is1-2+nx*(is2-2+ny*(is3-2))


2155: 


2156:       ai = power( a, nx, is2-2+ny*(is3-2), is1-2 )


2157:       d1 = ie1 - is1 + 1


2158:       e1 = ie1 - is1 + 2


2159:       e2 = ie2 - is2 + 2


2160:       e3 = ie3 - is3 + 2


2161:       x0 = x


2162:       rdummy = randlc( x0, ai )


2163:       do  i3 = 2, e3


2164:          x1 = x0


2165:          do  i2 = 2, e2


2166:             xx = x1


2167:             call vranlc( d1, xx, a, z( 2, i2, i3 ))


2168:             rdummy = randlc( x1, a1 )


2169:          enddo


2170:          rdummy = randlc( x0, a2 )


[...]


2182:          ten( i, 1 ) = 0.0D0


2183:          j1( i, 1 ) = 0


2184:          j2( i, 1 ) = 0


2185:          j3( i, 1 ) = 0


2186:          ten( i, 0 ) = 1.0D0


2187:          j1( i, 0 ) = 0


2188:          j2( i, 0 ) = 0


2189:          j3( i, 0 ) = 0


2190:       enddo


2191: 


2192:       do  i3=2,n3-1


2193:          do  i2=2,n2-1


2194:             do  i1=2,n1-1


2195:                if( z(i1,i2,i3) .gt. ten( 1, 1 ) )then


2196:                   ten(1,1) = z(i1,i2,i3) 


2197:                   j1(1,1) = i1


2198:                   j2(1,1) = i2


2199:                   j3(1,1) = i3


2200:                   call bubble( ten, j1, j2, j3, mm, 1 )


2201:                endif


2202:                if( z(i1,i2,i3) .lt. ten( 1, 0 ) )then


2203:                   ten(1,0) = z(i1,i2,i3) 


2204:                   j1(1,0) = i1


2205:                   j2(1,0) = i2


2206:                   j3(1,0) = i3


2207:                   call bubble( ten, j1, j2, j3, mm, 0 )


[...]


2220:       do  i=mm,1,-1


2221: 


2222:          best = z( j1(i1,1), j2(i1,1), j3(i1,1) )


2223:          call mpi_allreduce(best,temp,1,dp_type,  &


2224:      &        mpi_max,comm_work,ierr)


2225:          best = temp


2226:          if(best.eq.z(j1(i1,1),j2(i1,1),j3(i1,1)))then


2227:             jg( 0, i, 1) = me


2228:             jg( 1, i, 1) = is1 - 2 + j1( i1, 1 ) 


2229:             jg( 2, i, 1) = is2 - 2 + j2( i1, 1 ) 


2230:             jg( 3, i, 1) = is3 - 2 + j3( i1, 1 ) 


2231:             i1 = i1-1


2232:          else


2233:             jg( 0, i, 1) = 0


2234:             jg( 1, i, 1) = 0


2235:             jg( 2, i, 1) = 0


2236:             jg( 3, i, 1) = 0


2237:          endif


2238:          ten( i, 1 ) = best


2239:          call mpi_allreduce(jg(0,i,1), jg_temp,4,MPI_INTEGER,  &


2240:      &        mpi_max,comm_work,ierr)


2241:          jg( 0, i, 1) =  jg_temp(1)


2242:          jg( 1, i, 1) =  jg_temp(2)


2243:          jg( 2, i, 1) =  jg_temp(3)


2244:          jg( 3, i, 1) =  jg_temp(4)


2245:

2248:      &        mpi_min,comm_work,ierr)


2249:          best = temp


2250:          if(best.eq.z(j1(i0,0),j2(i0,0),j3(i0,0)))then


2251:             jg( 0, i, 0) = me


2252:             jg( 1, i, 0) = is1 - 2 + j1( i0, 0 ) 


2253:             jg( 2, i, 0) = is2 - 2 + j2( i0, 0 ) 


2254:             jg( 3, i, 0) = is3 - 2 + j3( i0, 0 ) 


2255:             i0 = i0-1


2256:          else


2257:             jg( 0, i, 0) = 0


2258:             jg( 1, i, 0) = 0


2259:             jg( 2, i, 0) = 0

2261:          endif

The code analyzed by CQA in that panel excludes loops and represents 0.00% of application time for run run_0
        

          
Source file and linesmg.f90:2119-2313

          
Modulemg_mpi_f90_aocc

        

        
The function is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-MPI/MG/mg.f90:2119,2149-2170,2182-2207,2220-2265,2293-2306,2313.

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 104.83 to 54.00 cycles (1.94x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function


Vectorization


Your function is poorly vectorized.
Only 21% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 104.83 to 71.44 cycles (1.47x speedup).

Details


47% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 53% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 12% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 54% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized function can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:

    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  

No data for this section

  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

  • mpi_barrier_@plt: 2 occurrences►

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-MPI/MG/mg.f90:2119
    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-MPI/MG/mg.f90:2119

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

  • LEA: 5 occurrences►

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-MPI/MG/mg.f90:2156

Workaround


Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Type of elements and instruction set

The binary function does not contain any FP arithmetical operations.
The binary function is loading 1116 bytes.
The binary function is storing 1104 bytes.

  • FP5 (256 bits): FP store data

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 104.83 cycles. At this rate:

  • 11% of peak load performance is reached (10.65 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 16% of peak store performance is reached (10.53 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 104.83 to 79.67 cycles (1.32x speedup).

The code analyzed by CQA in that panel excludes loops and represents 0.00% of application time for run run_0
        

          
Source file and linesmg.f90:2119-2313

          
Modulemg_mpi_f90_aocc

        

        
The function is defined in /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-MPI/MG/mg.f90:2119,2149-2170,2182-2207,2220-2265,2293-2306,2313.

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 104.83 to 54.00 cycles (1.94x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function


Vectorization


Your function is poorly vectorized.
Only 21% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 104.83 to 71.44 cycles (1.47x speedup).

Details


47% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 53% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 12% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 54% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized function can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:

    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  

No data for this section

  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

  • mpi_barrier_@plt: 2 occurrences►

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

  • LEA: 5 occurrences►

    • /home/ndkd/lab/ihpc/aoc/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-MPI/MG/mg.f90:2156

Workaround


Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 104.83 cycles. At this rate:

  • 11% of peak load performance is reached (10.65 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 16% of peak store performance is reached (10.53 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 104.83 to 79.67 cycles (1.32x speedup).
